standard, regional vocabulary aside. As with other varieties, however, there is American English. Standard written English is almost identical with the British pronunciation have many common features, and are closer to British than to (breakfast),  Tassie (Tasmania), arvo (afternoon). Australian and New  Zealand example barbie (barbeque), mushie (mushroom), sunnies (sunglasses), brekkie Australian English frequently shortens nouns and adds an -ie or -o suffix: for conversational she instead of it, as in She'll be right (It will be OK).   Informal questions like Will I call back later?, where British English has Shall I. . .?; and (The team is confident of winning); the New  Zealand use of Will I...? in verbs with group nouns, where British English might use a plural verb grammatical differences from British English include a preference for singular (genuine), bonzer (excellent), my shout (my turn to buy the drinks). The  few between the two varieties - for instance bludger (a lazy person), dinkum rich store of informal and slang words and expressions, many of them shared birds), mana (power, honour). Both Australian and New  Zealand English have a from the indigenous language Maori: for example pukeku and takahe (names of (a tree, wilderness). Similarly, New  Zealand English has incorporated words kangaroo, billabong (a pool or a tributary of a river), dingo (a wild dog), mulga for natural features from the Aboriginal languages of Australia: for example has a good deal of regional vocabulary.
